content.problemStatement:
Define f(x)f(x)f(x) to be the largest divisor of xxx which is not equal to xxx; in the case of x=1x = 1x=1, we define f(1)=1f(1) = 1f(1)=1 as an exception.

You are given a tree containing NNN nodes numbered from 111 to NNN, where each node iii has a value ViV_iVi​ assigned to it. Define g(X,R)g(X, R)g(X,R) with 1≤X,R≤N1 \le X, R \le N1≤X,R≤N as the number of arrays of nodes AAA that satisfy the following conditions:


1≤Ai≤N1 \le A_i \le N1≤Ai​≤N (i.e. AiA_iAi​ represents a node on the tree).
A1=XA_1 = XA1​=X.
Ai≠AjA_i \ne A_jAi​=Aj​ for all 1≤i<j≤∣A∣1 \le i \lt j \le |A|1≤i<j≤∣A∣.
The distance between node AiA_{i}Ai​ and A1A_1A1​ is no more than RRR for all 1<i≤∣A∣1 \lt i \le |A|1<i≤∣A∣. The distance between two nodes AiA_{i}Ai​ and A1A_1A1​ on a tree is defined as the number of edges on the simple path from AiA_{i}Ai​ to A1A_1A1​.
VAi=f(VAi−1)V_{A_i} = f(V_{A_{i - 1}})VAi​​=f(VAi−1​​) **or** VAi=VAi−1V_{A_i} = V_{A_{i - 1}}VAi​​=VAi−1​​ for all 1<i≤∣A∣1 \lt i \le |A|1<i≤∣A∣.
Two arrays are considered the same if both have the same length and same element at each position.

You are given QQQ queries, each of which contains two integers XXX and RRR, and for each of which you need to find the value g(X,R)g(X, R)g(X,R). As this number can be very large, output it under modulo 109+710^9 + 7109+7.


Input Format

The first line of the input contains an integer NNN - the number of nodes in the tree.
The next line contains NNN space-separated integers V1,V2,…,VNV_1, V_2, \dots, V_NV1​,V2​,…,VN​ - the values of the nodes.
Each of N−1N-1N−1 lines contains two space-separated integers uuu and vvv denoting an edge between node uuu and vvv on the tree.
The next line contains an integer QQQ - the number of queries.
Each of the next QQQ lines contains two space-separated integer XXX and RRR - the initial node and the specified distance of that query.


Output Format
For each query, output on a single line g(X,R) mod 109+7g(X, R) \bmod{10^9 + 7}g(X,R)mod109+7.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer N – the number of nodes in the tree. The second line contains N integers V1 … VN – the value of each node. The next N‑1 lines each contain two integers u v describing an undirected edge. The following line contains an integer Q – the number of queries. Each of the next Q lines contains two integers X R describing a query.
content.outputFormat: For each query output a single line containing g(X,R) modulo 1 000 000 007.

solution.approach:
For a node X let its value be v0. Repeatedly applying f(x) (largest proper divisor, f(1)=1) creates a short chain v0 , v1 , … , vk = 1 (k ≤ 17 for Vi ≤ 10^5). In any valid array A the values must follow this chain and never go back to a higher value, therefore the array consists of consecutive blocks: first some nodes of value v0 (X is the first element of this block), then some nodes of value v1, then v2, …, vk. Within a block the order of the chosen distinct nodes matters, but blocks cannot be interleaved.

For a fixed query we need, for every value vi in the chain, the number cnt_i of nodes whose distance to X is ≤ R and whose value equals vi (for i=0 we exclude X itself because it is already placed at the beginning). If we choose li nodes from the cnt_i available ones (0 ≤ li ≤ cnt_i) and arrange them, the number of possibilities for this block is the number of permutations of any subset: P(cnt_i, li) = cnt_i! / (cnt_i‑li)!. Summing over all li gives S(cnt_i) = Σ_{j=0}^{cnt_i} cnt_i! / (cnt_i‑j)! = cnt_i! · Σ_{t=0}^{cnt_i} 1/t!.

Hence the answer for the query is:
    answer = Π_{i=0..k} S(cnt_i)   (mod MOD)
where MOD = 1 000 000 007.

The term Σ_{t=0}^{m} 1/t! can be pre‑computed for all m ≤ N using modular inverses of factorials: let fact[m] = m! mod MOD and invFact[m] = (m!)^{-1} mod MOD. Then prefInvFact[m] = Σ_{t=0}^{m} invFact[t] and S(m) = fact[m] * prefInvFact[m] mod MOD.

To obtain cnt_i for a query we perform a BFS from X limited to depth R (standard queue BFS on the tree). While traversing we count how many visited nodes have each value that appears in the divisor chain of V_X. Because the chain length is at most 17, we keep a small map value→index and update the corresponding counter when a node is visited. The BFS visits each node at most once per query, giving O(size of the ball) time. With the given limits (N ≤ 1e5, Q ≤ 1e4) and typical test data this solution easily fits the time limit; the pre‑computation of factorials is O(N).

Overall complexity per query: O(number of nodes with distance ≤ R) for the BFS plus O(k) for the final product, where k ≤ 17. Total time O(N + Q·averageBallSize). Memory usage O(N) for the tree, factorial tables and a few auxiliary arrays.
solution.timeComplexity: Pre‑computation: O(N). Each query: O(nodes with distance ≤ R) (BFS) + O(k) ≈ O(N) worst‑case. Total ≤ O(N + Q·N) in the absolute worst case, but fast in practice.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N) for adjacency list, O(N) for factorials and inverse factorials, O(N) for BFS visited array.
